The text discusses the threat of poisoning attacks on recommender systems, which are integral parts of online services to help users find specific information. Poisoning attacks involve an adversary injecting manipulated data into the training process of a model to alter the system's final recommendations.

These attacks can significantly undermine the model's accuracy, leading to potential commercial consequences for the service providers. The article provides a taxonomy of poisoning attacks and reviews various countermeasures to detect and prevent these attacks, but their effectiveness against specific types of attacks is still unclear.

The text discusses the threat of poisoning attacks on recommender systems, such as Amazon and eBay, where a company can pay for fraudulent ratings to promote or demote their own products or those of competitors. The attacks can be categorized as AI-based or classic heuristic attacks.

AI-based attacks involve training an end-to-end framework to forge user profiles and imitate authentic user behavior, using techniques like generative adversarial networks (GANs) or exploiting the reward signal in a reinforcement learning scheme.

The text discusses a survey on poisoning attacks aimed at manipulating Recommender Systems, which provide recommendations based on user preferences. The attack mechanism involves injecting malicious data into the system's training data or temporarily modifying user data to bias the system's learning process and manipulate its recommendations. This can influence the recommendations shown to a user during their current session or impact the recommendations for a broader set of users over an extended period.

The text discusses the threat of poisoning attacks on recommender systems, which are widely used in platforms like YouTube, Netflix, Amazon, and Google Play. These systems analyze past user-item interactions to make recommendations. The text identifies five categories of recommender systems: matrix-factorization-based, graph-based, association-rule-based, neighborhood-based, and deep-learning-based.

In a poisoning attack, an adversary manipulates the training data of a machine learning model to corrupt its integrity. The goal is often to promote or demote specific items

The text discusses the threat of AI-based attacks, specifically poisoning attacks on recommender systems. These attacks involve introducing fake data (either manually or automatically) to manipulate the model's learning process, resulting in biased recommendations that serve the attacker's goals.

Poisoning attacks can be categorized as model-agnostic or model-intrinsic. Model-agnostic attacks are less effective as they ignore the specific model and algorithms used, while model-intrinsic attacks are optimized for a specific type of training process and can cause significant damage.
